title: Commercial Strip Mall FDC / Standpipe Supply

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
FDC Supply Line A: 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to building FDC
FDC Supply Line B: 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to building FDC
Interior attack line: 150 ft of 2.5-inch hose from standpipe outlet to nozzle
appliances: Building FDC / standpipe system, 25 PSI system loss
sceneElements.elevation: Grade-level commercial occupancy, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: The operation has one flowing interior attack line.
The nozzle is a 1 1/8-inch smooth bore.
FirePumpSim standard flow for a 1 1/8-inch smooth bore is 265 GPM.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total flow requirement is based on the flowing interior attack line supplied through the FDC / standpipe system. The 1 1/8-inch smooth bore line requires 265 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add flow for the two FDC supply hoses. Those hoses are supplying the same standpipe operation, not two separate attack lines.

problems.title: FDC Supply Line Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in each FDC supply line?
formulaBreakdown: The FDC is supplied by two equal supply lines.
Flow per FDC supply line = 265 ÷ 2 = 132.5 GPM.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
FDC supply line length = 100 ft = 1 hundred.
problems.instructorExplanation: Because the two FDC supply lines are equal, the total flow is split evenly between them. Each line carries 132.5 GPM, producing approximately 5 PSI friction loss.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate each FDC supply line using the full 265 GPM. The two equal lines split the flow before entering the FDC.

problems.title: FDC / Standpipe System Loss
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required for the building FDC / standpipe system?
formulaBreakdown: FirePumpSim standard FDC / standpipe system loss applies.
problems.instructorExplanation: The building FDC / standpipe system adds 25 PSI to the pump discharge pressure requirement.
problems.explainMistake: Do not treat the FDC connection as zero loss. The standpipe system loss must be added when calculating pump discharge pressure.

problems.title: Total Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FDC supply line friction loss = 5 PSI.
Interior attack line FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Interior attack line uses 2.5-inch hose, so C = 2.
Interior attack line length = 150 ft = 1.5 hundreds.
Round interior FL to nearest 5 PSI: 20 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pump must overcome the FDC supply hose friction loss, the interior attack line friction loss, nozzle pressure, and the FDC / standpipe system loss. The total required pump discharge pressure is 100 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate only the hose from the engine to the FDC. A complete standpipe supply calculation also includes the interior attack line, nozzle pressure, and standpipe system loss.

problems.title: Sprinkler System Only
problems.question: If the FDC is being used only to supply the building sprinkler system, what pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: For FirePumpSim sprinkler-only FDC support, use the standard sprinkler supply pressure unless a building placard or department SOP gives a different value.
Standard sprinkler-only FDC pump pressure = 150 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: When the FDC is being used only to support the sprinkler system, the FirePumpSim standard setting is 150 PSI. This is a sprinkler-system support pressure rather than an interior attack-line pump pressure calculation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate this as a standpipe handline operation. If the FDC is only supporting sprinklers, use the sprinkler-system FDC supply pressure unless a specific placard or local SOP states otherwise.
